text: Hello and welcome.
Today I'm sharing another robotic sigmoid colectomy, but this one in particular to demonstrate troubleshooting after a positive leak test.
So we have a middle-aged woman, multiple bouts of recurrent acute diverticulitis.
The surgical field reveals extensive scarring and adhesions in the pelvic region, indicative of a previous hysterectomy.
So it wasn't a terribly difficult case for diverticulitis. It was pretty straightforward.
I'm going to jump pretty quickly just because I want to get to the part with the positive leak test.
The surgeon carefully frees the diseased segment of the sigmoid colon from dense adhesions on the left pelvic side wall.
The surgeon uses all four robotic arms to provide retraction, enhancing the exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully peels adhesions off the ovary, revealing the underlying tissue.
I did have urology come in and inject ICG into the ureters, which are very helpful.
The surgeon selects the proximal resection margin on the colon, noting its flexibility, without needing to mobilize the splenic flexure.
The surgeon has mobilized the rectum extensively, ensuring it is free from adhesions to facilitate the anastomosis.
The surgeon identifies the intended distal resection margin but has not yet cleared the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon selects the proximal resection margin and carefully dissects through the mesentery, avoiding larger vessels to ensure a safe and straightforward approach.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to perform a mesenteric transection, progressing smoothly towards the distal resection margin.
Now I know I've done a lot of these cases that sometimes that mesentery is extremely thick. It's folded over on itself and that can make this part pretty challenging.

The surgeon works to straighten and flatten the mesentery folds, preparing to use the vessel sealer for transection without needing a lower dissection to fully mobilize the rectal mesentery from the sacrum.
The surgeon has cleaned off the distal resection margin at the level of the rectum, revealing a clear boundary for further surgical steps.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the rectum at the proximal site, performing partial cycles to avoid full dissection.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) dye to assess the perfusion of the tissue, confirming that the blood flow appears adequate.

The surgeon has transected the bowel and is using bowel sizers to ensure the rectum is straight for specimen removal. The specimen appears relatively compact and not overly thick, allowing for easier extraction without fragmentation.
The surgeon cleans the surgical area and passes a 29mm EEA stapler transanally. The stapler is placed proximally, and a purse string suture is applied to secure the tissue.

I've been doing this purse string suture different almost every time and I think now I've moved on to doing a true purse string and not the baseball stitch over and over because I really like to bunch up the tissue and have beefy anastomotic rings after the anastomosis
I don't think that doing the over and over stitch, whether you're going from inside out or outside in, quite gives you that perfect circle.
The surgeon performs a rectal purse string suture, demonstrating the technique in detail while encountering difficulties during the procedure.
The surgeon repeatedly stitches the tissue with small bites, ensuring the tissue remains bunched up without large gaps.
The surgeon cinches down the purse string suture, revealing an oblong and asymmetric shape rather than a perfect circle.
The surgeon passes the EDA stapler transanally and deploys the spike, while alternating between the robotic console to align and connect the tissue ends.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis using a rigid proxiscope, ensuring the instrument reaches up to and above the anastomotic site, with no visible tension on the anastomosis.
The surgeon inflates the area, revealing significant bubbling at the site, indicating a potential issue with the anastomosis.

So a positive leak test, but the beauty with the robotic platform is the visualization is so good. I always dreaded this happening when I would do these laparoscopically, because I feared being able to troubleshoot.

At this point, trying to decide, okay, what are my options here? Do I take down the anastomosis and redo it? I had enough length proximally, and I had enough rectum to do that if I thought it was necessary, and I would have had no hesitation to do that.
Or do I repair it primarily and repeat the leak test? And then if I do that, which I do, do I divert her?

And so taking everything into consideration here, I said, well, I'm going to start primarily repairing this and kind of see how it goes, see how it looks. If I feel good about it, I'll repeat that leak test.
And I know, because I used ICG again, that I have very good perfusion at the anastomosis. I probably will not divert her is what I'm thinking at this point.

So I could really see the defect well. I even kind of knew where it was going to be when I inspected my anastomotic rings. There was clearly a defect at the posterior aspect and one of my purse string sutures was not intact. So I kind of figured there was going to be a leak on the leak test and sure enough there was.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Loc absorbable suture to repair the defect, starting on the left side where the tissue is intact, and continues the suture across to the right side beyond the defect, ensuring the tissue is securely over-sewn.

So I feel pretty good about that, but I got very aggressive with my leak test here. I wanted to have no doubt. leaving the OR without diverting her that I felt confident about the anastomosis. So there's no tension, there's good perfusion, and a vigorous negative leak test at this point. So I did not divert her. And I, of course, you know, waited a while before posting this. She is doing very well. So thank you very much for tuning in.
